facts.text: "The name's Bond... James Bond" — Sean Connery first delivered this iconic introduction in Dr. No (1962), establishing the character's suave persona forever.
Ian Fleming created James Bond in 1952 while staying at his Goldeneye estate in Jamaica, writing the first novel Casino Royale in just two months.
Fleming named his spy after the American ornithologist James Bond, whose book Birds of the West Indies sat on Fleming's desk in Jamaica.
Dr. No (1962) was the first official James Bond film, produced by Albert R. Broccoli and Harry Saltzman under their EON Productions banner.
Sean Connery was a relatively unknown Scottish actor when he was cast as Bond, beating out over 200 other candidates for the role.
The iconic Aston Martin DB5 made its first appearance in Goldfinger (1964) and has since appeared in more Bond films than any other car.
"Do you expect me to talk?" "No, Mr. Bond, I expect you to die!" — This exchange between Bond and Auric Goldfinger is one of cinema's most quoted villain lines.
The gun barrel sequence that opens every Bond film was designed by Maurice Binder and first appeared in Dr. No.
Ursula Andress emerging from the sea in a white bikini in Dr. No is considered one of the most iconic moments in cinema history.
"A martini. Shaken, not stirred." — Bond's signature drink order became a cultural catchphrase, though Fleming's novels originally had Bond ordering his martinis stirred.
The James Bond franchise is the longest-running film series in history, spanning over 60 years of continuous production.
Q Branch, the fictional research and development division of MI6, has provided Bond with gadgets in nearly every film since From Russia with Love (1963).
Desmond Llewelyn portrayed Q in 17 Bond films from 1963 to 1999, making him the most prolific actor in the franchise after Bond himself.
"I never joke about my work, 007." — Desmond Llewelyn's Q delivered this line to establish the character's serious yet comedic dynamic with Bond.
From Russia with Love (1963) featured one of the greatest fight scenes in cinema history, a brutal brawl between Bond and Red Grant on the Orient Express.
President John F. Kennedy listed From Russia with Love as one of his ten favorite books, helping boost the Bond franchise's popularity in America.
Goldfinger (1964) was the first Bond film to earn over $100 million worldwide and established the template for all future Bond adventures.
Shirley Bassey recorded three Bond theme songs: Goldfinger, Diamonds Are Forever, and Moonraker, more than any other artist.
The Aston Martin DB5 in Goldfinger was equipped with an ejector seat, machine guns, a bulletproof shield, tire slashers, and an oil slick dispenser.
Gert Fröbe, who played Goldfinger, spoke limited English and had his dialogue entirely dubbed in post-production by actor Michael Collins.
"Shocking. Positively shocking." — Sean Connery's Bond quipped after electrocuting an assassin in the bathtub in Goldfinger.
Thunderball (1965) was the highest-grossing Bond film when adjusted for inflation, earning the equivalent of over $1 billion in today's money.
The underwater battle scenes in Thunderball required the largest underwater filming crew ever assembled at the time.
You Only Live Twice (1967) was filmed extensively in Japan and featured a hollowed-out volcano as Blofeld's secret lair, designed by Ken Adam.
Ken Adam's legendary set designs for the Bond films influenced countless other productions, including the Star Wars franchise.

"Mr. Bond, they have a saying in Chicago: once is happenstance, twice is coincidence, the third time it's enemy action." — Auric Goldfinger's menacing observation from Fleming's novel became a famous maxim.
Sean Connery reportedly grew tired of the role after five films and initially left the franchise after You Only Live Twice.
George Lazenby became the second James Bond in On Her Majesty's Secret Service (1969), despite having no prior acting experience.
On Her Majesty's Secret Service is widely regarded by critics as one of the finest Bond films, featuring Bond's only marriage in the official series.
"We have all the time in the world." — George Lazenby's Bond says this to his new bride Tracy just before she is tragically killed by Blofeld.
Louis Armstrong recorded 'We Have All the Time in the World' for On Her Majesty's Secret Service; it was one of his last recordings before his death.
George Lazenby chose not to return for a second Bond film, reportedly advised by his agent that the spy genre was dying out.
Sean Connery returned for one more official Bond film, Diamonds Are Forever (1971), donating his entire salary to charity.

"My dear girl, there are some things that just aren't done, such as drinking Dom Pérignon '53 above the temperature of 38 degrees Fahrenheit." — Connery's Bond schooled a companion on champagne etiquette in Goldfinger.
Roger Moore took over the role of Bond in Live and Let Die (1973) and would go on to star in seven films, the most of any Bond actor.
Paul McCartney and Wings performed the title song for Live and Let Die, which became one of the best-selling Bond theme songs of all time.
Roger Moore was 45 years old when he first played Bond and 57 when he made his final appearance in A View to a Kill (1985).
The Spy Who Loved Me (1977) featured the iconic Lotus Esprit submarine car, which Elon Musk later purchased at auction for nearly $1 million.
"Nobody Does It Better" by Carly Simon, the theme from The Spy Who Loved Me, was the first Bond theme not to share the film's title.
Richard Kiel played the 7-foot-2 steel-toothed villain Jaws in The Spy Who Loved Me and Moonraker, becoming one of Bond's most popular adversaries.
Moonraker (1979) sent Bond into outer space during the height of Star Wars fever, featuring a laser battle in a space station.
"He's not the Messiah, he's a very naughty boy" was nearly parodied in Moonraker's marketing, showing how the franchise flirted with camp in the Moore era.
The pre-title sequence of The Spy Who Loved Me features Bond skiing off a cliff and deploying a Union Jack parachute, regularly voted the greatest Bond stunt ever.
For Your Eyes Only (1981) deliberately scaled back the over-the-top elements of the Moore era, returning to a more realistic espionage tone.
Octopussy (1983) was released the same year as the unofficial Bond film Never Say Never Again, creating a so-called 'Battle of the Bonds' at the box office.
Never Say Never Again (1983) saw Sean Connery return as Bond one final time in a non-EON production remake of Thunderball.
A View to a Kill (1985) featured Christopher Walken as villain Max Zorin and the Duran Duran title song, which hit number one on the Billboard Hot 100.
"I'm afraid you've caught me with more than my hands up." — Roger Moore's version of Bond was known for his cheeky one-liners and double entendres.
Timothy Dalton took over as Bond in The Living Daylights (1987), bringing a darker, grittier interpretation closer to Fleming's original novels.
The Living Daylights featured the Aston Martin V8 Vantage with a laser beam, retractable outriggers, and a jet propulsion system.
Licence to Kill (1989) was the first Bond film to receive a PG-13 rating in the United States due to its increased violence and darker tone.
Timothy Dalton's portrayal of Bond is often credited as a precursor to Daniel Craig's more serious and emotionally complex interpretation.
"Stuff my orders! I only kill professionals. That girl didn't know one end of a rifle from the other." — Timothy Dalton's Bond showed a more conflicted moral compass.
A six-year gap between Licence to Kill and GoldenEye was the longest hiatus in Bond history, caused by legal disputes over the franchise rights.
Pierce Brosnan was originally offered the role of Bond in 1986 but had to decline due to his contractual obligations to the TV series Remington Steele.
GoldenEye (1995) marked Pierce Brosnan's debut as Bond and revitalized the franchise for a new generation, earning $350 million worldwide.
Judi Dench debuted as M in GoldenEye, making her the first woman to play Bond's boss, and she continued in the role for seven films.
"I think you're a sexist, misogynist dinosaur, a relic of the Cold War." — Judi Dench's M famously dressed down Bond in GoldenEye, redefining their dynamic.
The GoldenEye 007 video game for the Nintendo 64 (1997) became one of the most influential first-person shooters in gaming history.
The tank chase through St. Petersburg in GoldenEye is one of the most beloved action sequences in the franchise.
Tomorrow Never Dies (1997) featured Michelle Yeoh as Wai Lin, one of Bond's most capable and equal female counterparts.
The World Is Not Enough (1999) took its title from the Bond family motto, 'Orbis non sufficit,' which appeared in On Her Majesty's Secret Service.
"The world is not enough." "Foolish sentiment." "Family motto." — This exchange between Bond and Elektra King revealed the deeper meaning of the film's title.
Die Another Day (2002) celebrated the franchise's 40th anniversary with numerous references to previous films but received mixed reviews for its heavy use of CGI.
Halle Berry's emergence from the ocean in Die Another Day was a deliberate homage to Ursula Andress's iconic scene in Dr. No.
"So you're a killer." "I'm not—" "Oh, yes you are. It's in your eyes, the coldness, a certain arrogance." — Vesper Lynd's cutting assessment of Bond in Casino Royale (2006) became instantly iconic.
Daniel Craig was announced as the sixth Bond in October 2005, initially facing significant fan backlash before Casino Royale silenced all critics.
Casino Royale (2006) rebooted the franchise, showing Bond earning his 00 status and presenting a raw, vulnerable version of the character.
The poker game in Casino Royale replaced the baccarat of Fleming's novel with Texas Hold'em, considered more accessible to modern audiences.
Casino Royale's brutal stairwell fight scene was inspired by the Bourne films and signaled a new era of visceral, realistic Bond action.
The opening parkour chase in Casino Royale, featuring Sébastien Foucan, is considered one of the greatest action sequences in Bond history.
"I'm the money." "Every penny of it." — The flirtatious exchange between Vesper Lynd and Bond on the train in Casino Royale crackled with chemistry.
Eva Green's portrayal of Vesper Lynd in Casino Royale is frequently cited as the greatest Bond girl performance in the franchise's history.
Quantum of Solace (2008) was the first direct sequel in Bond history, picking up just minutes after Casino Royale ended.
Skyfall (2012) became the first Bond film to earn over $1 billion at the worldwide box office, coinciding with the franchise's 50th anniversary.
"Last rat standing." — Silva's rat analogy in Skyfall, delivered by Javier Bardem, is one of the most chilling villain monologues in Bond history.
Adele's 'Skyfall' won the Academy Award for Best Original Song, the first Bond theme to win an Oscar since 1981.
Skyfall's cinematographer Roger Deakins earned universal praise for the film's stunning visuals, particularly the Shanghai skyscraper fight filmed in silhouette.
The destruction of the Aston Martin DB5 in Skyfall was so controversial that the car was rebuilt and returned in subsequent films.
"Some men are coming to kill us. We're going to kill them first." — Daniel Craig's Bond delivers this blunt line in Skyfall, encapsulating Craig's no-nonsense portrayal.
Skyfall explored Bond's childhood origins, revealing that he grew up as an orphan at the Skyfall estate in Scotland.
Spectre (2015) brought back the iconic villain organization SPECTRE, which had been absent from the official films for decades due to legal issues.
Christoph Waltz played Ernst Stavro Blofeld in Spectre, revealing that the villain was Bond's adoptive brother in a controversial plot twist.
"It was me, James. The author of all your pain." — Christoph Waltz's Blofeld taunted Bond in Spectre with this menacing line.
The opening Day of the Dead tracking shot in Spectre in Mexico City was one of the longest continuous shots in Bond history.
No Time to Die (2021) was Daniel Craig's fifth and final Bond film, giving the character an unprecedented emotional and definitive ending.
"The world's not going to save itself." — Bond's simple justification for returning to service in No Time to Die resonated with the franchise's core theme.
Billie Eilish became the youngest artist to record a Bond theme song, performing 'No Time to Die' at age 18, and won the Oscar for it.
No Time to Die was delayed multiple times due to the COVID-19 pandemic, moving from April 2020 to October 2021.
Lashana Lynch became the first Black woman to hold the 007 designation in No Time to Die, though Bond himself retained his identity.
Rami Malek played the villain Safin in No Time to Die, a role he described as 'the culmination of my wildest dreams as an actor.'
Ana de Armas received widespread acclaim for her brief but memorable role as Paloma in No Time to Die, despite only having about 10 minutes of screen time.
"You don't know what it's like to have your heart broken because you still have one." — Bond's words to Madeleine Swann in No Time to Die showed his emotional evolution.
Ian Fleming wrote 12 James Bond novels and 2 short story collections between 1953 and his death in 1964.

Fleming's Bond novels, in order, are Casino Royale, Live and Let Die, Moonraker, Diamonds Are Forever, From Russia with Love, Dr. No, Goldfinger, Thunderball, The Spy Who Loved Me, On Her Majesty's Secret Service, You Only Live Twice, and The Man with the Golden Gun.
Fleming based many Bond characters on real people he met during his time in British Naval Intelligence during World War II.
Fleming's wartime boss, Rear Admiral John Godfrey, is widely believed to be the inspiration for M, Bond's superior at MI6.
The character of Q was based on Charles Fraser-Smith, who supplied real gadgets to British agents during WWII.
Fleming wrote each Bond novel at his Goldeneye estate in Jamaica, typically completing a first draft in about six weeks.
The fictional MI6 headquarters at 85 Vauxhall Cross in London is actually the real-life location of the Secret Intelligence Service.
Bond's code number 007 was inspired by the 007 bus route Fleming took in Canterbury and the Elizabethan spy John Dee, who signed his letters to Queen Elizabeth I with '007.'
"Bond. James Bond." has been voted the greatest movie one-liner of all time in multiple polls, beating out lines from Star Wars and The Godfather.
SPECTRE stands for Special Executive for Counter-intelligence, Terrorism, Revenge and Extortion, one of fiction's most elaborate villain organization names.
Ernst Stavro Blofeld, the head of SPECTRE, has been portrayed by more actors than any other Bond villain, including Donald Pleasence, Telly Savalas, and Charles Gray.
Donald Pleasence's portrayal of Blofeld in You Only Live Twice, complete with facial scar and white cat, directly inspired the Dr. Evil character in Austin Powers.
The recurring image of Blofeld stroking a white Persian cat has become one of the most parodied villain tropes in cinema history.
"Twice?" "I'll kill you if you don't." — Rosa Klebb's shoe with a poisoned blade in From Russia with Love introduced one of Bond's most creative villain weapons.
Oddjob, Goldfinger's mute henchman known for his lethal bowler hat, was played by professional wrestler Harold Sakata.
Harold Sakata suffered real burns during the filming of Goldfinger's climax at Fort Knox but refused to break character.
Jaws, the steel-toothed giant played by Richard Kiel, was so popular with audiences that he was brought back for a second film and given a love interest in Moonraker.
"Mr. Bond, you persist in defying my efforts to provide an amusing death for you." — Hugo Drax's exasperated line in Moonraker is quintessential Bond villain dialogue.
Max Zorin in A View to a Kill was one of the few Bond villains explicitly stated to be a product of Nazi genetic experimentation.
Javier Bardem's Raoul Silva in Skyfall is considered one of the greatest Bond villains, a former MI6 agent seeking revenge against M.
"Mommy was very bad." — Silva's unsettling reference to M in Skyfall highlighted the film's exploration of betrayal and maternal themes.
Francisco Scaramanga, the Man with the Golden Gun, was played by Christopher Lee, who was Ian Fleming's step-cousin in real life.
Christopher Lee had actually lobbied Fleming personally to play a Bond villain, and Fleming reportedly considered him for the role of Dr. No.
The golden gun itself was assembled from a pen, lighter, cigarette case, and cufflink — one of the franchise's most creative weapon designs.
Le Chiffre, the villain of Casino Royale, was played by Mads Mikkelsen, who made the character's bleeding eye a memorable physical trait.

"You know, I never understood all these elaborate tortures. It's the simplest thing... to cause more pain than a man can possibly endure." — Le Chiffre's torture scene with Bond became one of the franchise's most intense moments.
Bond villain lairs have included hollowed-out volcanoes, underwater cities, space stations, supertankers, and private islands.
Ken Adam designed many of the most iconic villain lairs, including the War Room in Dr. Strangelove, blurring the line between his Bond work and Kubrick collaborations.
Miss Moneypenny, M's secretary, has appeared in nearly every Bond film, maintaining a flirtatious but unconsummated relationship with 007.
Lois Maxwell played the original Miss Moneypenny in 14 Bond films from 1962 to 1985, establishing the character's playful chemistry with Bond.
"Moneypenny, what would I do without you?" "As far as I can remember, James, you've never had me." — This signature exchange captured the Moneypenny-Bond dynamic perfectly.
Naomie Harris reinvented Moneypenny for the Daniel Craig era, first appearing in Skyfall as a field agent before transitioning to her classic desk role.
Felix Leiter, Bond's CIA ally, has been played by more different actors than any other recurring Bond character, with eight actors in the role.
Jeffrey Wright brought consistency to Felix Leiter by playing the character across three Daniel Craig films, the first actor to reprise the role.
The Aston Martin DB5 used in Goldfinger was sold at auction in 2010 for $4.6 million, making it one of the most expensive film cars ever sold.
A total of four Aston Martin DB5s were used during the original Goldfinger production: two for stunts and two for close-up filming.

The Aston Martin DB5 has appeared in nine official Bond films: Goldfinger, Thunderball, GoldenEye, Tomorrow Never Dies, Casino Royale, Skyfall, Spectre, No Time to Die, and a brief cameo in The World Is Not Enough.
Bond's car collection across the franchise includes models from Aston Martin, Lotus, BMW, Toyota, AMC, Bentley, and Sunbeam.
The Lotus Esprit submarine car from The Spy Who Loved Me was nicknamed 'Wet Nellie' in homage to Little Nellie, the autogyro in You Only Live Twice.
BMW provided Bond's vehicles for three Pierce Brosnan films, a controversial product placement deal reportedly worth $100 million.
The Toyota 2000GT convertible driven in You Only Live Twice was custom-built because Sean Connery was too tall to fit in the standard hardtop model.
Bond drove an AMC Hornet in The Man with the Golden Gun, during which stuntman Bumps Willard performed a record-breaking 360-degree corkscrew jump over a river.
The corkscrew car jump in The Man with the Golden Gun was calculated by computer — one of the first uses of computer simulation in stunt planning.
In the novels, Bond's preferred car is a Bentley, not an Aston Martin; the switch was made for the films beginning with Goldfinger.
Bond's Aston Martin V12 Vanquish in Die Another Day featured an invisibility cloak, one of the franchise's most fantastical gadget upgrades.
"Now pay attention, 007." — Q's classic opening line before demonstrating a new gadget became one of the franchise's most anticipated recurring moments.
Q's gadgets have included jet packs, laser watches, explosive pens, grappling belt buckles, and a bagpipe flamethrower.
The jet pack used in Thunderball's pre-title sequence was a real Bell Rocket Belt, one of only a few ever built, flown by pilot Bill Suitor.
Ben Whishaw took over the role of Q starting with Skyfall, portraying a younger, tech-savvy version of the quartermaster.
"Were you expecting an exploding pen? We don't really go in for that anymore." — Ben Whishaw's Q wryly referenced past Bond gadgets in Skyfall.
Little Nellie, the autogyro in You Only Live Twice, was a real aircraft — the Wallis WA-116 — flown by its inventor Wing Commander Ken Wallis during filming.
The crocodile submarine in Octopussy was a real functional prop that Roger Moore actually piloted during filming in India.
Bond's Omega Seamaster watch replaced the Rolex Submariner starting with GoldenEye, beginning one of cinema's most successful product placement partnerships.
In Fleming's novels, Bond wears a Rolex Submariner, and the switch to Omega for the films was a commercial decision made in the 1990s.
"Oh, grow up, 007." — M's exasperated rebuke to Bond appeared in multiple films, reflecting the franchise's self-awareness about its hero's antics.
John Barry composed the scores for 11 Bond films, defining the franchise's musical identity with lush orchestral arrangements and brassy themes.
The James Bond Theme, composed by Monty Norman and arranged by John Barry, is one of the most recognizable pieces of film music ever created.
There has been a longstanding legal dispute over the authorship of the James Bond Theme, with both Monty Norman and John Barry claiming credit.
Shirley Bassey's performance of 'Goldfinger' is widely considered the definitive Bond theme song, setting the standard for all that followed.
Tom Jones reportedly fainted after hitting the final high note while recording the Thunderball theme song.
Nancy Sinatra's 'You Only Live Twice' was one of the most ethereal Bond themes, setting a template for the dreamlike quality of later entries.
'Live and Let Die' by Paul McCartney and Wings has remained in McCartney's concert setlist for decades, one of his most performed songs.
a-ha's 'The Living Daylights' blended the Norwegian band's synth-pop style with John Barry's orchestration, creating one of the most distinctive Bond themes.
Tina Turner's 'GoldenEye' was written by Bono and The Edge of U2, blending rock grandeur with Bond's signature brass.
Garbage performed 'The World Is Not Enough,' a theme that Shirley Manson delivered with the dramatic flair worthy of the franchise's tradition.
Madonna's 'Die Another Day' was the most controversial Bond theme at the time of its release, blending electronica in a way that divided fans.
Chris Cornell wrote and performed 'You Know My Name' for Casino Royale, a rock-driven theme that matched the film's grittier tone.
Jack White and Alicia Keys duetted on 'Another Way to Die' for Quantum of Solace, the first Bond theme performed as a duet.
Sam Smith's 'Writing's on the Wall' from Spectre won the Academy Award for Best Original Song, though it divided Bond fans.
Billie Eilish's 'No Time to Die' was written with her brother Finneas and won the Oscar, making them the youngest songwriting team to win for a Bond theme.
Carly Simon's 'Nobody Does It Better' remains the highest-charting Bond theme on the Billboard Hot 100, peaking at number two.
Sheena Easton's 'For Your Eyes Only' reached number four on the Hot 100, and she remains the only Bond theme singer to appear in the title sequence performing the song.
Radiohead recorded a rejected theme song for Spectre before Sam Smith's 'Writing's on the Wall' was chosen, later releasing it as 'Spectre.'
The Bond franchise has filmed in over 50 countries, making it one of the most geographically diverse film series ever produced.
Jamaica has featured prominently in multiple Bond films, reflecting Ian Fleming's deep personal connection to the island.
The famous Bond beach in Dr. No is Laughing Waters Beach in Ocho Rios, Jamaica, now a popular tourist destination.
Istanbul features prominently in From Russia with Love and Skyfall, with the Grand Bazaar and Hagia Sophia serving as iconic backdrops.
The ice hotel in Die Another Day was inspired by the real ICEHOTEL in Jukkasjärvi, Sweden.
Skyfall was extensively filmed in Scotland, featuring Glen Coe and the A82 road as the dramatic landscape surrounding Bond's childhood home.
The Italian city of Matera served as a key filming location in No Time to Die, with Bond's Aston Martin DB5 roaring through its ancient streets.
The Piz Gloria revolving restaurant atop Switzerland's Schilthorn mountain served as Blofeld's allergy research clinic in On Her Majesty's Secret Service and remains a Bond tourist attraction.
Pinewood Studios near London has been the home base of Bond film production since Dr. No, with the famous 007 Stage built specifically for The Spy Who Loved Me.
The 007 Stage at Pinewood Studios was one of the largest sound stages in the world when built in 1976, measuring 336 feet long and 139 feet wide.
The Bahamas has been a frequent Bond filming location, appearing in Thunderball, Casino Royale, and other films.
Venice's canals and architecture featured memorably in Moonraker, Casino Royale, and From Russia with Love.
The car chase through the narrow streets of Siena, Italy in Quantum of Solace was filmed during the actual Palio horse race festival.
"I thought Christmas only comes once a year." — Pierce Brosnan's Bond delivered this groan-worthy pun about Dr. Christmas Jones in The World Is Not Enough.
Rio de Janeiro's Sugarloaf Mountain cable car was the setting for Jaws's memorable fight with Bond in Moonraker.
The Rock of Gibraltar's military tunnels served as the opening location for The Living Daylights.
Thailand, specifically the Phang Nga Bay limestone karsts, became known as 'James Bond Island' after The Man with the Golden Gun.
Bregenz, Austria's floating opera stage was the setting for the memorable Tosca scene in Quantum of Solace.
Bond films have featured ski chases in the Alps more than any other type of terrain-specific action sequence.
The pre-title ski jump in The Spy Who Loved Me was performed by stuntman Rick Sylvester, who was paid $30,000 for the single jump.
The bobsled chase on the Cresta Run in On Her Majesty's Secret Service featured real bobsled footage mixed with studio shots.
Albert R. 'Cubby' Broccoli co-founded EON Productions and oversaw the Bond franchise from Dr. No until his death in 1996.
EON Productions gets its name from 'Everything or Nothing,' reflecting Cubby Broccoli's all-or-nothing approach to filmmaking.
After Cubby Broccoli's death, his daughter Barbara Broccoli and stepson Michael G. Wilson took over as producers of the Bond franchise.
Barbara Broccoli has stated that James Bond will always be portrayed as a male character, despite ongoing debates about the role's future.
Harry Saltzman co-produced the first nine Bond films with Broccoli before selling his share of the franchise in 1975 due to financial difficulties.
The legal battle over Bond film rights between EON Productions, Kevin McClory, and others lasted decades and affected which elements could appear in the films.
Kevin McClory's legal claim to the Thunderball story rights resulted in the non-EON film Never Say Never Again and limited EON's use of SPECTRE and Blofeld for years.
The Danjaq LLC holding company controls the Bond franchise on behalf of the Broccoli family, named after the wives of Broccoli and Saltzman (Dana and Jacqueline).
MGM has distributed Bond films for decades, and Amazon's acquisition of MGM in 2022 raised questions about the franchise's future direction.
"Vodka martini." "Shaken or stirred?" "Do I look like I give a damn?" — Daniel Craig's Bond dismissed the classic drink ritual in Casino Royale, signaling a new era.
In Casino Royale (2006), Bond orders the Vesper, a cocktail he invents and names after Vesper Lynd: three measures of Gordon's gin, one of vodka, and half a measure of Kina Lillet.
The Vesper cocktail from Casino Royale became a popular real-world cocktail, though bartenders typically substitute Lillet Blanc for the discontinued Kina Lillet.
Bond's preferred champagne has varied across films but most frequently includes Bollinger, which has been the official champagne of the franchise since the 1970s.
In the novels, Bond smokes 60 to 70 custom-made Morland cigarettes per day, a habit largely removed from the films as social attitudes toward smoking changed.
Bond's cigarettes in the early films transitioned from custom Turkish blends to more commercially available brands before the character largely stopped smoking by the 1990s.

"The distance between insanity and genius is measured only by success." — Elliot Carver's media mogul philosophy in Tomorrow Never Dies reflected the film's prescient commentary on media manipulation.
Roger Moore holds the record for the oldest actor to play Bond, making A View to a Kill at age 57.
George Lazenby, at age 29 during On Her Majesty's Secret Service, was the youngest actor to play Bond in an official EON film.
Daniel Craig is the longest-serving Bond actor in terms of years, holding the role from 2006 to 2021, spanning 15 years.
Sean Connery's Bond was ranked the best ever by most fans in polls, though Daniel Craig's tenure significantly closed the gap.
Roger Moore admitted in his autobiography that he felt he was too old for the role in his later Bond films, joking that his stunt double was getting old too.
Timothy Dalton had been offered the role of Bond as early as 1968 but turned it down, feeling he was too young at 22, before finally accepting in 1986.
"In my movies, Bond walks into a room and instantly women want him and men want to be him." — Pierce Brosnan described the essence of Bond's appeal in interviews.
Pierce Brosnan learned he had been fired as Bond while filming the Mamma Mia! sequel, having expected to make a fifth Bond film.
Daniel Craig initially turned down the role of Bond three times before producer Barbara Broccoli personally convinced him to accept.
Daniel Craig underwent a rigorous physical transformation for Casino Royale, emerging from the ocean in a scene that became as iconic as Ursula Andress's original.
The six official Bond actors — Connery, Lazenby, Moore, Dalton, Brosnan, and Craig — represent a total of 25 films spanning 59 years.
Casino Royale was first adapted in 1954 as a one-hour CBS television episode starring Barry Nelson as an American version of James Bond named 'Jimmy Bond.'
The 1967 Casino Royale was a comedic spoof featuring an ensemble cast including Peter Sellers, Woody Allen, and David Niven as a retired Bond.
"That's a Smith & Wesson, and you've had your six." — Connery's Bond dispatched Professor Dent in Dr. No with cold-blooded efficiency never quite replicated.
James Bond has been portrayed in officially authorized continuation novels by authors including Kingsley Amis, John Gardner, Raymond Benson, Sebastian Faulks, William Boyd, and Anthony Horowitz.
John Gardner wrote 14 Bond novels between 1981 and 1996, the most of any continuation author.
Anthony Horowitz's Bond novel Trigger Mortis incorporated an unpublished Ian Fleming short story, connecting modern continuation fiction to the original author.
Charlie Higson wrote a series of Young Bond novels exploring James Bond's teenage years at Eton College before he joined MI6.
"The scent and smoke and sweat of a casino are nauseating at three in the morning." — The opening line of Fleming's Casino Royale set the tone for Bond's world from the very first sentence.
Fleming's Bond novels established 007's tastes in detail: scrambled eggs made with a specific recipe, blue Crème de la Mer soap, and Sea Island cotton shirts.
In the novels, Bond is described as a tall, dark-haired man with a scar on his right cheek and cold blue-grey eyes, resembling the composer Hoagy Carmichael.
Fleming's Bond was half-Scottish and half-Swiss, with his parents Andrew Bond and Monique Delacroix dying in a climbing accident when he was 11.
Bond's obituary in You Only Live Twice reveals he attended Eton briefly before being removed and completing his education at Fettes College in Edinburgh.
"I shall not waste my days in trying to prolong them. I shall use my time." — Fleming attributed this philosophy to Bond, borrowed from a Jack London quote.
The Bond franchise has earned over $7 billion at the worldwide box office, making it one of the highest-grossing film series of all time.
When adjusted for inflation, the Bond franchise has earned the equivalent of over $16 billion in today's money.
Skyfall remains the highest-grossing Bond film in nominal terms, earning $1.109 billion worldwide in 2012.
Casino Royale (2006) is the highest-rated Bond film on Rotten Tomatoes among critics, with a 95% approval rating.
Bond films have been nominated for numerous Academy Awards, primarily in technical categories, winning Best Sound Editing, Best Visual Effects, and Best Original Song.
The Bond franchise has never received an Academy Award nomination for Best Picture, despite several films being considered worthy by critics.
"I admire your courage, Miss...?" "Trench. Sylvia Trench." — The first Bond girl introduced herself in Dr. No, mirroring Bond's iconic introduction.
Honor Blackman's Pussy Galore in Goldfinger was one of the first strong, independent Bond women, and her character's name caused controversy with censors.
Honor Blackman was already famous as Cathy Gale in The Avengers TV series when she was cast in Goldfinger, bringing genuine toughness to the role.
Diana Rigg, who played Tracy di Vicenzo in On Her Majesty's Secret Service, was also an Avengers alumna, having played Emma Peel.
Tracy di Vicenzo, played by Diana Rigg, is the only woman Bond married in the official film series, before her tragic death at Blofeld's hands.
Maud Adams is the only actress to play two different Bond girls in the official series, appearing in The Man with the Golden Gun and Octopussy.
Grace Jones's May Day in A View to a Kill was one of the most physically imposing Bond antagonists, matching Bond in combat before switching allegiances.
Famke Janssen's Xenia Onatopp in GoldenEye was one of the franchise's most memorable villainesses, known for her lethal pleasure-seeking methods.
"No, no, no. No more foreplay." — Xenia Onatopp's menacing line to Bond in GoldenEye captured her dangerous fusion of seduction and violence.
Denise Richards's nuclear physicist Dr. Christmas Jones in The World Is Not Enough is frequently cited as the least convincing Bond girl casting in franchise history.
Olga Kurylenko's Camille in Quantum of Solace was notable for being one of the few Bond women who didn't become a love interest, pursuing her own revenge arc instead.
Léa Seydoux's Madeleine Swann appeared in two Bond films, Spectre and No Time to Die, making her one of the most significant Bond women in the Daniel Craig era.
"If you don't trust me, what are we doing?" — Madeleine Swann's question to Bond in No Time to Die captured the emotional stakes of their relationship.
Fleming originally modeled the character of Vesper Lynd on Krystyna Skarbek, a real-life Polish SOE agent who was one of Britain's most decorated WWII spies.

The DB5's ejector seat in Goldfinger was triggered when Bond hit a hidden red button in the gear shift, prompting 007 to quip, "Ejector seat? You're joking." Q replied, "I never joke about my work, 007."
Stunt coordinator Vic Armstrong doubled for three different Bond actors — Connery, Moore, and Craig — holding a Guinness World Record for the most prolific stunt career.
The opening crane fight in Casino Royale was filmed in Madagascar and featured Daniel Craig performing many of his own stunts despite the dangerous heights.
Bond's iconic Walther PPK pistol was introduced in Dr. No when M ordered 007 to replace his Beretta, which had jammed on a previous mission.
"I asked you for a weapon, not a piece of art." — Bond's reaction to Q's gadget briefings often highlighted the tension between aesthetic and practical concerns.
The Walther PPK was replaced by the larger Walther P99 in Tomorrow Never Dies before returning to the PPK in subsequent films.
The pre-title sequences in Bond films were introduced in From Russia with Love and have become one of the franchise's signature structural elements.
GoldenEye's bungee jump from the top of the Contra Dam in Switzerland was voted the best movie stunt of all time in a 2002 Sky Movies poll.
The bungee jump was performed by stuntman Wayne Michaels, who leaped 720 feet from the top of the Verzasca Dam.
In Licence to Kill, the aerial stunt of Bond water-skiing behind a plane was performed without CGI, using real aircraft and practical effects.
The Aston Martin DBS in Casino Royale set a Guinness World Record for the most cannon rolls in a car — seven complete barrel rolls during the road crash.
"You want to know what's wrong with those American Secret Service agents? They don't learn foreign languages." — Bond's multilingual abilities were a consistent trait from Fleming's novels.
Bond speaks at least five languages fluently, including English, French, German, Russian, and Japanese, according to various films and novels.
The 'Bond girl' naming convention has included characters called Honey Ryder, Pussy Galore, Holly Goodhead, Octopussy, and Xenia Onatopp.
Ian Fleming enjoyed creating suggestive character names, a tradition the films continued with names like Plenty O'Toole and Dr. Christmas Jones.
"My name is Pussy Galore." "I must be dreaming." — Bond's reaction to meeting Goldfinger's pilot remains one of the franchise's wittiest exchanges.
Blofeld's first appearance was purely vocal in From Russia with Love, where he was seen only from the neck down stroking his white cat.
The full reveal of Blofeld's face didn't occur until You Only Live Twice (1967), when Donald Pleasence portrayed the scarred villain.
Telly Savalas played Blofeld in On Her Majesty's Secret Service, bringing a more physical and athletic interpretation to the character.
Charles Gray's Blofeld in Diamonds Are Forever was the campiest version, a far cry from the menacing villain of earlier films.
"World domination. Same old dream." — Bond's dismissive attitude toward Blofeld's ambitions in Dr. No encapsulated the franchise's humor about megalomaniacal villains.
The volcano set in You Only Live Twice, designed by Ken Adam, cost $1 million to build in 1967 — more than the entire budget of Dr. No.
Dr. No's metal hands were a distinctive feature that influenced the portrayal of Bond villains with physical deformities or disfigurements throughout the franchise.
Red Grant, played by Robert Shaw in From Russia with Love, is considered one of the most realistic and threatening Bond villains — a trained killer with no supernatural abilities.
"The first one won't kill you. Not the second. Not even the third. But the fourth will." — Red Grant's methodical threat to Bond on the Orient Express was terrifying in its precision.
Emilio Largo in Thunderball wore a black eye patch, adding to the gallery of physically distinctive Bond villains that Fleming loved to create.
Baron Samedi, the voodoo-inspired villain in Live and Let Die, was played by Geoffrey Holder, who improvised the character's distinctive laugh.
"Names is for tombstones, baby." — Mr. Big's chilling line in Live and Let Die reflected the film's blaxploitation-era influences.
Elektra King in The World Is Not Enough was the first main Bond villain to be female in the official film series.
"There's no point in living if you can't feel alive." — Elektra King's philosophy in The World Is Not Enough blurred the line between villain and sympathetic character.
The Bond films have grossed more in aggregate than any other film franchise when counting only the main series of 25 official films.
The tradition of a Bond-themed cold open followed by a title sequence with silhouetted women was pioneered by graphic designer Maurice Binder.
Maurice Binder created the title sequences for 14 Bond films, establishing the franchise's visual language of silhouettes, gunplay, and color.
Daniel Kleinman took over title sequence design from Binder starting with GoldenEye and has designed every Bond title sequence since.
The 007 logo, with a stylized gun forming the 7, was designed by Joseph Caroff for the Dr. No marketing campaign.
Bond's tuxedo has become as iconic as the character himself, with Tom Ford designing Daniel Craig's suits in the later films.
In Goldfinger, Jill Masterson is killed by being painted entirely in gold paint, causing 'skin suffocation' — a medically inaccurate but cinematically unforgettable death.
Shirley Eaton, who played Jill Masterson, has said she had to leave a small patch of skin unpainted during the gold scene due to safety concerns.
"A gold-plated girl... murdered by gold paint." — Bond's discovery of Jill Masterson's body in Goldfinger remains one of the franchise's most striking images.
The laser scene in Goldfinger, where a laser beam threatens to bisect Bond, was achieved using a practical effect with a man beneath the table cutting the metal with a blowtorch.
Bond's gambling skills are a recurring theme, from baccarat in Dr. No and Thunderball to poker in Casino Royale and craps in Diamonds Are Forever.
The Texas Hold'em poker tournament in Casino Royale had a buy-in of $10 million, with the final pot reaching over $115 million.
Professional poker player Tom Sambrook served as Daniel Craig's poker advisor during the filming of Casino Royale.
"That last hand nearly killed me." — Bond's poker quip in Casino Royale worked on multiple levels after he'd been poisoned during the game.
The Casino Royale poker scenes were choreographed by poker experts to ensure the hands were statistically plausible.
The opening poker game on the yacht in Casino Royale (1967 spoof) was a far cry from the tense, realistic poker of the 2006 version.
"Well, I understand double-0s have a very short life expectancy." — M's comment to the newly promoted Bond in Casino Royale underscored the danger of the 00 program.
In Fleming's universe, there are only three active double-0 agents at any time, each with a licence to kill.
The double-0 designation gives agents the authority to kill in the line of duty, a concept that was considered shocking when Fleming first introduced it.
MI6, formally the Secret Intelligence Service (SIS), is Britain's real foreign intelligence agency and has acknowledged Bond's influence on public perception of the service.
The real MI6 has used Bond's popularity as a recruitment tool, with former MI6 chief Sir John Sawers acknowledging the 'Bond effect' on applicants.
"For England, James?" "No. For me." — The exchange between Alec Trevelyan and Bond in GoldenEye perfectly captured Craig-era loyalty themes years before Craig took the role.
Alec Trevelyan (006) in GoldenEye, played by Sean Bean, was one of the franchise's most personal villains as a former friend and colleague of Bond.
Sean Bean's Trevelyan was the first villain to be a fellow 00 agent, adding a layer of betrayal that deepened the film's emotional stakes.
The phrase 'shaken, not stirred' appears in Fleming's novels, but Bond actually says 'stirred, not shaken' in the novel Casino Royale.
Bond's drink preferences in the novels also include bourbon (specifically Jack Daniel's), sake, and Dom Pérignon champagne.
The Bond franchise has inspired countless parodies, including the Austin Powers trilogy, Johnny English, the Kingsman films, and the animated series Archer.
Mike Myers' Dr. Evil in Austin Powers was a direct parody of Donald Pleasence's Blofeld, complete with a bald head, scar, and scheme for world domination.
"Do you expect me to talk?" "No, Mr. Powers, I expect you to die!" — Austin Powers directly parodied the famous Goldfinger exchange, highlighting Bond's cultural penetration.
'M' stands for the head of MI6 in both the Bond universe and real life, where the initial was traditionally used by the organization's chief.
Bernard Lee played the original M in 11 Bond films before his death in 1981, establishing the character's gruff, paternal relationship with 007.
Robert Brown succeeded Bernard Lee as M, appearing in four Bond films during the Timothy Dalton and late Roger Moore eras.
Judi Dench's M was one of the most significant characters in Bond history, spanning seven films across two different Bond actors.
"Take the bloody shot!" — M's command in Skyfall's pre-title sequence demonstrated the impossible choices the character had to make.
Ralph Fiennes took over as M in Skyfall and has continued in the role, bringing a different authoritative presence to the character.
"Now I know what C stands for." — Ralph Fiennes' M got one of the biggest laughs in Spectre with this dismissive putdown of Andrew Scott's villain.
The relationship between M and Bond evolved significantly across the franchise, from a simple superior-subordinate dynamic to the complex maternal bond with Judi Dench's M.
Judi Dench's death scene as M in Skyfall is one of the most emotional moments in Bond history, with 007 holding her as she dies in his arms.
"I did get one thing right." — M's final words to Bond in Skyfall, acknowledging that trusting him was her greatest achievement.
The cold open of The Spy Who Loved Me was kept secret from Roger Moore — he reportedly gasped when he first saw the Union Jack parachute footage.
The Spy Who Loved Me is the only Bond film novel not adapted from Fleming's story; he only allowed the title to be used, requiring an entirely original screenplay.
Live and Let Die was the first Bond film to use a significant Black cast and explore Black culture, though its depiction has been criticized as stereotypical.
The crocodile farm scene in Live and Let Die, where Bond runs across crocodile backs, was performed by crocodile farm owner Ross Kananga — after whom the villain Kananga was named.
Moonraker holds the record for the most expensive Bond film of the Roger Moore era, costing $34 million (approximately $140 million adjusted for inflation).
"In space, no one can hear you scream" was a tagline for Alien released the same year as Moonraker, and Bond's space adventure felt like a response to the sci-fi boom.
The Man with the Golden Gun (1974) was filmed partly in the ruins of a half-constructed Hong Kong hotel, giving it a distinctive unfinished aesthetic.
Scaramanga's third nipple in The Man with the Golden Gun was described in Fleming's novel as a sign of supernatural power in certain Eastern cultures.
The Man with the Golden Gun is often cited as one of the weakest Bond films, despite Christopher Lee's commanding performance as the villain.
"Sit down and shut up." — Roger Moore's Bond could shift from gentlemanly charm to cold authority in an instant.
For Your Eyes Only featured a cameo by a character resembling Blofeld (though never named due to legal restrictions), who Bond unceremoniously dumps into a smokestack.
Octopussy featured Bond disguised as a clown to infiltrate a circus, one of the most divisive creative choices in the franchise's history.
"No well-dressed man should be without one." — Bond's quip about his various gadgets became a running joke reflecting the character's sartorial obsession.
The Living Daylights featured the Aston Martin V8 Vantage in one of the franchise's most exciting car chases through snow-covered Czechoslovakia.
Licence to Kill drew inspiration from the real-world drug trade of the 1980s, making its villain Franz Sanchez feel more grounded than typical Bond antagonists.
Robert Davi's Franz Sanchez in Licence to Kill was one of the most ruthless Bond villains, feeding a man to sharks and using a decompression chamber as a torture device.
"Señor Bond, you got big cojones." — Franz Sanchez's backhanded compliment in Licence to Kill was blunt in a way few Bond villains managed.
The six-year gap between Licence to Kill and GoldenEye was largely caused by MGM's financial difficulties and legal battles over the franchise rights.
GoldenEye was the first Bond film produced entirely after the Cold War ended, forcing the franchise to find new relevance in a changed geopolitical landscape.
"Unlike the American government, we prefer not to get our bad news from CNN." — M's dry wit in GoldenEye addressed the post-Cold War world head-on.
The GoldenEye satellite weapon in the film was an EMP device, reflecting real Cold War-era fears about electromagnetic pulse attacks disabling electronics.
Tomorrow Never Dies depicted a media mogul manipulating world events for ratings — a plot that seems increasingly prescient with each passing year.
Jonathan Pryce's Elliot Carver in Tomorrow Never Dies was reportedly inspired by real-world media magnates like Rupert Murdoch and Robert Maxwell.
"There's no news like bad news." — Elliot Carver's media philosophy in Tomorrow Never Dies anticipated the 24-hour news cycle's most cynical tendencies.
The World Is Not Enough featured Robbie Coltrane as Valentin Zukovsky, Bond's frenemy, who added humor and depth in both that film and GoldenEye.
Denise Richards' casting as a nuclear physicist named Christmas Jones in The World Is Not Enough was widely mocked, though Richards has defended the role.
Die Another Day's invisible car, achieved through 'adaptive camouflage,' was considered the franchise's most unrealistic gadget since Moonraker's laser-armed space shuttle.
Die Another Day opened with Bond being captured, tortured, and imprisoned in North Korea for 14 months — the first time a Bond film showed 007 genuinely broken.
"So you're a killer." — Vesper Lynd's unflinching characterization of Bond in Casino Royale immediately established that this was a different kind of Bond film.
Daniel Craig broke two of his teeth during the stairwell fight scene in Casino Royale, but continued filming.
The sinking building in Venice at the climax of Casino Royale was a full-scale set built at Pinewood Studios, flooded with controlled water effects.
"The bitch is dead." — Bond's cold final line about Vesper in Casino Royale shocked audiences with its brutality and emotional complexity.
Quantum of Solace takes its title from a Fleming short story about a dinner party anecdote, the least action-oriented source material in the franchise.
Quantum of Solace was affected by the 2007-2008 Writers Guild strike, reportedly resulting in Daniel Craig and director Marc Forster rewriting scenes themselves.
"I don't think the dead care about vengeance." — Bond's line in Quantum of Solace showed a character processing grief in a way earlier Bonds never explored.
Skyfall director Sam Mendes is the only Academy Award-winning Best Director to helm a Bond film (he won for American Beauty in 1999).
Skyfall deliberately echoed themes from The Dark Knight, with Sam Mendes acknowledging Christopher Nolan's influence on the Bond franchise's darker direction.
The exploding MI6 headquarters in Skyfall was one of the franchise's most shocking moments, bringing the threat literally to Bond's doorstep.
Albert Finney played Kincade, the gamekeeper at the Skyfall estate, a role originally written with Sean Connery in mind.
"Sometimes the old ways are the best." — Kincade's line in Skyfall while preparing booby traps served as the film's thesis about the relevance of traditional spycraft.
The Shanghai fight scene in Skyfall, filmed entirely in silhouette against neon jellyfish screens, is considered one of the most beautifully shot action sequences in any Bond film.
Skyfall's Komodo dragon pit scene at the Macau casino paid homage to classic Bond villain traps while adding a visceral new threat.
Spectre's car chase through Rome between the Aston Martin DB10 and the Jaguar C-X75 was filmed over several nights on closed-off Roman streets.
The Aston Martin DB10 in Spectre was designed exclusively for the film, with only 10 units built, of which 8 were used for filming.
Spectre revealed that all previous Daniel Craig-era villains — Le Chiffre, Mr. White, Silva, and Greene — were connected through the SPECTRE organization.
Monica Bellucci became the oldest Bond girl at age 50 when she appeared in Spectre as Lucia Sciarra, the widow of a SPECTRE operative.
"You're a kite dancing in a hurricane, Mr. Bond." — Mr. White's warning in Spectre painted a vivid picture of Bond's vulnerability against SPECTRE.
Dave Bautista's Mr. Hinx in Spectre was a mostly silent antagonist in the tradition of Oddjob and Jaws, communicating menace through sheer physical presence.
The train fight between Bond and Mr. Hinx in Spectre was one of the most brutal hand-to-hand combat scenes in the franchise's history.
No Time to Die's villain Safin was inspired by elements of several Fleming villains, and his mask-wearing appearance added an eerie quality unprecedented in Bond films.
No Time to Die made Bond a father for the first time in the official series, with his daughter Mathilde adding unprecedented emotional stakes.
The ending of No Time to Die, in which Bond sacrifices his life to save his family, was the most emotionally devastating conclusion in Bond history.
"I know. But she'll know I loved her." — Bond's final communication with Moneypenny in No Time to Die, referring to Madeleine, brought audiences to tears.
Daniel Craig reportedly insisted that Bond die in No Time to Die, wanting to give the character a definitive ending for his era.
No Time to Die's opening sequence in Matera, Italy, featured a breathtaking motorcycle stunt where Bond jumps off a bridge, remounting the bike mid-air.
The Aston Martin DB5's machine guns were fully deployed in No Time to Die's Matera sequence, marking the first time Bond used every weapon on the car simultaneously.
Phoebe Waller-Bridge was brought in to polish the No Time to Die script, adding sharper dialogue and stronger character moments.
Cary Joji Fukunaga directed No Time to Die, the first American director to helm an official Bond film.
"You could be the death of me." "You're not going to die." "You don't know that." — Bond and Madeleine's exchange in No Time to Die foreshadowed the film's tragic ending.
The Bond franchise has generated an estimated $20 billion in total revenue when including merchandise, home video, and licensing.
Bond's Walther PPK was introduced on Major Boothroyd's recommendation in Dr. No, replacing Bond's Beretta 418, which M called 'a ladies' gun.'
"Tell me, does the whole world know you're a spy?" — Madeleine Swann's exasperated question in Spectre highlighted Bond's conspicuously unconcealed lifestyle.
The Bond franchise has featured real-world luxury brands throughout its history, including Aston Martin, Omega, Bollinger, Tom Ford, and Heineken.
The controversial Heineken product placement in Skyfall reportedly earned the production $45 million, sparking debate about commercialism in the franchise.
Bond's hotel preferences in the films have included the Ritz, Claridge's, the Atlantis Paradise Island, and numerous fictional establishments.
"I take pleasure in great beauty." — Bond's appreciation for the finer things, from wine to women to architecture, has been a consistent character trait across all eras.
The tradition of billing Bond actors with 'Ian Fleming's James Bond 007' was established early in the franchise's marketing.
Casino Royale (2006) was the first Bond film to show Bond earning his 00 status, depicted in a stark black-and-white pre-credits sequence.
The two kills required for 00 status were shown in Casino Royale's bathroom fight scene, one messy and one clinically efficient.
"Made you feel it, did he?" — Bond's second kill in the Casino Royale prologue was delivered with chilling composure, establishing Craig's Bond as genuinely dangerous.
The Bond films have collectively won five Academy Awards: three for Sound/Sound Editing and two for Best Original Song.
John Barry's Bond scores blended jazz, orchestral, and pop elements in ways that defined film music in the 1960s and 1970s.
David Arnold scored five Bond films from Tomorrow Never Dies to Quantum of Solace, becoming the most prolific Bond composer after John Barry.
Thomas Newman composed the scores for Skyfall and Spectre, bringing a more atmospheric and introspective musical approach to the franchise.
Hans Zimmer scored No Time to Die, marking the first time the legendary composer worked on a Bond film.
The Bond theme guitar riff, often attributed to John Barry, was actually played by session guitarist Vic Flick on a 1939 Clifford Essex Paragon Deluxe.
"This never happened to the other fella." — George Lazenby's Bond broke the fourth wall in On Her Majesty's Secret Service, the only time Bond acknowledged previous actors.
The tradition of Bond returning in the end credits began with Dr. No's 'James Bond will return in From Russia with Love' and has continued in every film.
The end credits promise 'James Bond will return' has appeared in every official Bond film, though the next film's title isn't always included.
During the 25 official films, Bond has visited at least 60 different countries or territories.
Bond's British passport in the films lists his birthday as either November 11 or April 13, depending on the source.
Ian Fleming died on August 12, 1964, at age 56, just as the Bond film franchise was becoming a global phenomenon with Goldfinger.
Fleming lived to see the first two Bond films — Dr. No and From Russia with Love — succeed at the box office before his death.
"I'm not an actor, I'm a movie star!" — Sean Connery's quote about the distinction reflected the larger-than-life quality Bond required.
Sean Connery's discomfort with the Bond role led him to wear a toupee in every Bond film, as he had begun losing his hair before Dr. No.
The underwater sequences in Thunderball were filmed by Ricou Browning, who had previously played the Gill Man in Creature from the Black Lagoon.
Diamonds Are Forever was set primarily in Las Vegas, making it one of the few Bond films to spend most of its runtime in the United States.
"Exceptionally fine shot." "I didn't know there was a pool down there." — Bond's quips after killing villains became a franchise trademark during the Moore and Brosnan eras.
Roger Moore once stated that his favorite of his own Bond films was The Spy Who Loved Me, praising its balance of action, humor, and spectacle.
The underwater city of Atlantis in The Spy Who Loved Me was one of Ken Adam's most ambitious set designs, creating a villain lair inside a supertanker.
For Your Eyes Only's opening scene, featuring a wheelchair-bound Blofeld-like villain being dropped into a smokestack, was a petty response to the legal dispute over the character.
The Union Jack parachute from The Spy Who Loved Me has been used as a symbol of British cinema and was referenced in the 2012 Olympics opening ceremony featuring Daniel Craig as Bond.
During the 2012 London Olympics opening ceremony, Daniel Craig's Bond appeared to parachute into the stadium alongside Queen Elizabeth II in a memorable short film.
Queen Elizabeth II gamely participated in the Olympics Bond sketch, leading many to declare her the greatest Bond girl of all time.
"Good evening, Mr. Bond." — The Queen's only line in the Olympics sketch became one of the most-quoted moments of the 2012 ceremonies.
The Goldfinger laser scene was groundbreaking for its time; lasers were a brand-new technology in 1964, and most audiences had never seen one before.
"Choose your next witticism carefully, Mr. Bond, it may be your last." — Goldfinger's threat during the laser scene was all the more menacing for its politeness.
Bond's cover identities throughout the franchise have included a marine biologist, a genealogist, a banker, and various military personnel.
In You Only Live Twice, Bond 'died' and was buried at sea, then underwent cosmetic surgery to appear Japanese — one of the franchise's most dated and controversial plot points.
The hollowed-out volcano lair in You Only Live Twice cost so much that its construction was reportedly spotted by a real spy satellite, with intelligence agencies briefly concerned.
The speedboat chase through the Louisiana bayous in Live and Let Die set a Guinness World Record for the most boats destroyed in a single stunt sequence.
"Just a slight stiffness coming on." — Roger Moore's Bond delivered sexual innuendo with such gentlemanly reserve that it became his defining characteristic.
Timothy Dalton studied Fleming's novels extensively before playing Bond, determined to bring a literary accuracy to his portrayal.
"If he fires me, I'll thank him for it." — Timothy Dalton's Bond showed contempt for bureaucracy, reflecting Fleming's original character's disdain for desk work.
The Kenworth tanker truck chase in Licence to Kill, where Bond tilts the truck onto one side to avoid a missile, remains one of the franchise's most impressive practical stunts.
Pierce Brosnan's first scene as Bond in GoldenEye — driving a stolen tank through St. Petersburg — immediately established his unique blend of charm and destruction.
The GoldenEye bunker in Puerto Rico, where the climatic antenna battle takes place, was filmed at the Arecibo Observatory radio telescope.
The Arecibo Observatory, which served as the GoldenEye villain's lair, collapsed in December 2020, adding poignancy to filmgoers' memories.
"For England, James?" "No. For me." — The final exchange between Trevelyan and Bond atop the Arecibo dish is one of the franchise's most emotionally resonant climaxes.
Tomorrow Never Dies featured a remote-controlled BMW 750iL chase through a parking garage, controlled via Bond's Ericsson phone — a gadget that anticipated smartphone technology.
The HALO (High Altitude Low Opening) jump that opens Tomorrow Never Dies was one of the most complex aerial stunts in Bond history, filmed by four skydiving cameramen.
"I could have given you the world." "The world is not enough." "Foolish sentiment." "Family motto." — This exchange in The World Is Not Enough doubled as product placement for the franchise itself.
Die Another Day's invisible car was achieved using 'adaptive camouflage,' technology that the US military has since actually researched for real vehicles.
Die Another Day grossed $431 million worldwide, making it the highest-grossing Bond film at that time, despite mixed critical reception.
The surf scene in Die Another Day that featured obvious CGI of Bond surfing a tsunami was widely criticized and helped push the franchise toward the reboot with Casino Royale.
Casino Royale's black-and-white opening was a deliberate artistic choice to separate the reboot from the colorful, fantastical elements of the Brosnan era.
The stairwell fight in Casino Royale was filmed in a real stairwell in Prague, with minimal camera tricks, showing Bond's kills as brutal and unglamorous.
Mads Mikkelsen prepared for his role as Le Chiffre by learning to weep blood from his left eye using a prosthetic contact lens.
"Arrogance and self-awareness seldom go hand in hand." — Bond's observation in Casino Royale was one of many quotable lines in the franchise's sharpest screenplay.
Judi Dench was the only actor to appear in both the Brosnan and Craig eras, providing continuity during the franchise's reboot.
The sinking house in Casino Royale's Venice climax required 450 crew members and four months to build, only to be destroyed in a single take.
Quantum of Solace's rapid editing and shaky camera work were intentional stylistic choices by director Marc Forster, though they divided audiences and critics.
The Tosca opera scene in Quantum of Solace, where Bond eavesdrops on QUANTUM members via their earpieces, is considered one of the Craig era's most inventive sequences.
Mathieu Amalric played Dominic Greene in Quantum of Solace, a villain whose scheme to control Bolivia's water supply felt more plausible than typical Bond villainy.
"Vesper." — Bond's first line in Quantum of Solace, spoken after capturing Mr. White, immediately established that the film was driven by his grief over Vesper Lynd.
Skyfall was the first Bond film to be shot digitally, using the ARRI Alexa camera system, a choice championed by cinematographer Roger Deakins.
The Macau casino sequence in Skyfall, lit by Chinese lanterns and candlelight, is one of the most visually sumptuous scenes in any Bond film.
Javier Bardem was reportedly reluctant to play Silva in Skyfall until Sam Mendes flew to Barcelona to personally convince him.
Silva's cyberterrorism in Skyfall reflected real-world concerns about digital warfare, making him one of the most contemporary Bond villains.
"Think on your sins." — Silva's message to M in Skyfall was a chilling prelude to his vendetta against her.
The Skyfall estate in Scotland was a full-scale set built on the Hankley Common in Surrey, England, disguised to look like the Scottish Highlands.
Skyfall's climax, a siege on the remote Scottish estate, was a deliberate homage to Sam Peckinpah's Straw Dogs and the 'last stand' tradition in Westerns.
"Everyone needs a hobby." "What's yours?" "Resurrection." — Bond's dry exchange in Skyfall after returning from presumed death showed Craig's mastery of deadpan humor.
Spectre's single-take opening in Mexico City's Day of the Dead parade required 1,500 extras, months of planning, and a custom-built giant skeleton float.
The helicopter fight over the Zócalo in Spectre's opening was performed by real stunt pilots with actors on wires inside the aircraft.
"The dead are alive." — Spectre's opening title card set an ominous tone connecting the past to the present.
The meteor crater villain lair in Spectre was built as a full-scale set at Pinewood Studios, one of the largest sets ever constructed for a Bond film.
Léa Seydoux was cast in Spectre after previously auditioning for a role in Skyfall, making her long association with the franchise a happy accident.
No Time to Die's production was troubled, with original director Danny Boyle departing over creative differences about the film's ending.
Danny Boyle reportedly wanted Bond to survive No Time to Die, while the producers and Craig favored a definitive ending — a disagreement that led to Boyle's departure.
The missile strike that kills Bond at the end of No Time to Die made him the first 007 to die in an official EON film.
"History isn't kind to men who play God." — Bond's warning in No Time to Die echoed the franchise's longstanding theme of human folly in seeking absolute power.
The Heracles bioweapon in No Time to Die — nanobots programmed to kill specific DNA targets — was considered one of the franchise's most scientifically grounded villain plots.
Bond's relationship with his daughter Mathilde in No Time to Die was deliberately understated, with Bond never explicitly telling her he's her father during the film.
The title 'No Time to Die' was interpreted by fans as a reference to Bond's mortality, his dedication to duty, and the franchise's own refusal to die after six decades.
After Daniel Craig's departure, the search for the seventh James Bond has been one of the most speculated-about casting decisions in entertainment history.
Idris Elba, Tom Hardy, Henry Cavill, and Regé-Jean Page have all been frequently mentioned as potential Bond candidates, though no announcement has been made.
Barbara Broccoli has stated that casting the next Bond won't begin until a creative direction for the franchise's future is determined.
"Bond is always evolving. He's not a museum piece." — Barbara Broccoli has consistently emphasized that each new Bond must reflect the era in which he appears.
The James Bond franchise has survived the Cold War, the fall of the Berlin Wall, 9/11, the rise of social media, and a global pandemic — adapting its storytelling each time.
Ian Fleming's Bond novels have sold over 100 million copies worldwide, making them among the best-selling book series in literary history.
Bond's influence on the spy genre is immeasurable, directly inspiring Mission: Impossible, the Bourne series, Kingsman, The Man from U.N.C.L.E., and countless other franchises.
"The world has changed, but Bond hasn't." — This criticism, leveled at the franchise periodically, has been answered each time the series reinvented itself with a new actor and tone.
Bond uses a Beretta 418 in the first five Fleming novels before switching to the Walther PPK in Dr. No, a change suggested by firearms expert Geoffrey Boothroyd.
The real Geoffrey Boothroyd wrote to Fleming criticizing Bond's choice of the Beretta, leading Fleming to rename the MI6 armorer 'Major Boothroyd' in his honor.
The Bond franchise pioneered the modern blockbuster merchandising model, with tie-in toys, games, clothing, and branded products appearing from the earliest films.
The James Bond theme has been sampled and referenced in hundreds of songs, commercials, and cultural moments since its 1962 debut.
"World enough and time." — The Bond franchise has drawn on literary references from Shakespeare to Marvell, reflecting Fleming's own well-read sensibility.
Sylvia Trench, who appeared in Dr. No and From Russia with Love, was originally planned as a recurring girlfriend for Bond, but the idea was dropped after two films.
The briefcase in From Russia with Love, containing a folding rifle, gold coins, and a tear gas canister, was one of the first iconic Q Branch gadgets.
Honey Ryder, played by Ursula Andress in Dr. No, set the template for the Bond girl — beautiful, resourceful, and ultimately rescued by 007.
Ursula Andress's voice was dubbed in Dr. No by actress Nikki van der Zyl, who went on to dub multiple Bond women in early franchise films.
The tarantula scene in Dr. No, where a spider crawls across Bond in his bed, was one of the franchise's earliest suspense sequences.
"You were supposed to get the girl." — The tradition of Bond ending each film with a romantic moment with the lead woman was established from the very first film.
Diamonds Are Forever featured a moon buggy chase through the Nevada desert, one of the franchise's most surreal action sequences.
The elevator fight in Diamonds Are Forever between Bond and smugglers Peter Franks was one of Sean Connery's most physical scenes in the series.
"Ah, you mean this sort of thing?" — Q's demonstrations of gadgets, often resulting in comical chaos in the background, became beloved running gags.
The Bond franchise has featured submarines, aircraft carriers, space shuttles, hovercraft, gondolas, and even a cello case sled as modes of transport.
Thunderball's underwater scenes required 20 days of underwater filming in the Bahamas, an unprecedented technical achievement in 1965.
The jetpack in Thunderball inspired generations of engineers, and the Bell Rocket Belt used in the film is now displayed in the Smithsonian Institution.
SPECTRE Island, the fictional training ground in From Russia with Love, was actually filmed on location at Pinewood Studios' exterior lot.
The orient Express featured prominently in From Russia with Love, with Bond's journey from Istanbul to Venice forming the film's entire second act.
"Red wine with fish. That should have told me something." — Bond identified Red Grant as an impostor in From Russia with Love through his wine faux pas.
The chess match in From Russia with Love, based on a real 1960 game between Boris Spassky and David Bronstein, established the intellectual dimension of Bond's world.
The belly dancer fight in From Russia with Love was choreographed by the same team that would later work on the Beatles' film Help!
Bill Tanner, M's Chief of Staff, appears in the novels and several films, most prominently played by Rory Kinnear in the Daniel Craig era.
"You know Bond. James Bond." — Characters across the franchise have recognized Bond by reputation, making his supposed status as a secret agent ironically well-known.
The Bond franchise has continued to push practical stunts over CGI, with No Time to Die featuring over 8,000 extras and minimal digital enhancement for its action scenes.
The Norwegian town of Nittedal doubled as a Russian location in No Time to Die, where Madeleine Swann's childhood home was set.
James Bond comic strips ran in the Daily Express newspaper from 1958 to 1983, adapting the novels and creating original stories.
The Bond films have been translated into dozens of languages and released in virtually every country on Earth, making 007 a truly global cultural icon.

"I always thought M was a randomly assigned letter. I had no idea it stood for..." "Utter one more syllable and I'll have you killed." — Bond and M's exchange in Casino Royale was Judi Dench at her deadliest.
Bond's loyalty to Queen and Country has been a defining characteristic, though the Craig era complicated this by showing a Bond willing to go rogue when necessary.
The tradition of Bond films opening at Christmas or in autumn became standard starting in the 1990s, making 007 a holiday season staple.
"Keeping the British end up, sir." — Roger Moore's Bond said this while in bed with a woman, epitomizing his era's reliance on innuendo.
Sean Connery was knighted in 2000, becoming Sir Sean Connery, though he had left the Bond role decades earlier.
Roger Moore was knighted in 2003, making two former Bond actors knights of the British Empire.
Timothy Dalton went on to acclaimed work in British theater and television after Bond, including a memorable turn in Hot Fuzz.
Pierce Brosnan became an environmental activist after leaving the Bond franchise, using his celebrity to advocate for ocean conservation.
Daniel Craig received the honorary rank of Commander in the Royal Navy in 2021, matching Bond's fictional naval rank exactly.
"The name is Bond, James Bond" was ranked the number one movie quote by the American Film Institute in their list of greatest movie quotes.
The recurring motif of Bond visiting exotic casinos reflects Fleming's own love of gambling, particularly at the Le Touquet casino in France.
Casino scenes appear in Dr. No, Thunderball, On Her Majesty's Secret Service, Diamonds Are Forever, The Man with the Golden Gun, GoldenEye, Casino Royale, and Skyfall.
Fleming's description of Bond's card playing established baccarat as the character's preferred casino game, though the films have varied this.
The baccarat game in Casino Royale (the novel) was changed to poker for the 2006 film because audiences were more familiar with Texas Hold'em.
Bond has been parodied in over 200 films and television shows, making him perhaps the most spoofed fictional character in cinema history.
Our Man Flint (1966) and In Like Flint (1967) were among the earliest Bond spoofs, starring James Coburn as a super-spy even more capable than 007.
The term 'Bond Girl' has become part of the English lexicon, though modern films have moved toward more empowered female characters and the term 'Bond Woman.'
"You want me to be half-monk, half-hitman." — Daniel Craig's Bond expressed the impossible duality of the character's existence in Skyfall.
The franchise has featured numerous real-world locations as MI6 headquarters, from the fictional Universal Exports cover to the real Vauxhall Cross building.
The Universal Exports cover company used by MI6 in the films was a creation of the screenwriters, not from Fleming's novels.
Bond's office at MI6 is rarely shown in the films, though it appears briefly in several entries, typically featuring a sparse, military-style desk.
"Ours is not to reason why." — Bond's occasional Tennyson references reflected the literary education befitting a spy who attended Eton and Fettes.
